parsed.reasoning: The bug is that create_config() assigns config directly to the DEFAULTS dictionary, so updates mutate the shared global defaults and affect future calls. I refactored it to return a shallow copy of DE
create_config mutates and returns the shared DEFAULTS dictionary instead of returning an independent copy, so overrides leak into future configs and reset behavior is fragile.

parsed.reasoning: The stale data comes from two places: app.py caches the settings object, and config.py returns the DEFAULTS dictionary by reference, so updates can mutate shared state and future reads can appear stal
The config loader mutates and reuses the DEFAULTS object plus a cached settings object, so later changes can appear stale or accidentally shared across calls.

The bug is caused by shared mutable state. create_config() returns the DEFAULTS dictionary by reference, and ConfigMiddleware caches that same object in _base. Then apply_config() mutates the cached d
Config state is shared across requests because create_config and ConfigMiddleware both return/mutate the same underlying dict reference instead of constructing a fresh per-request copy.

The profile update logic violated the invariant that display_name must always match name. I refactored the update function for clarity by handling supported fields explicitly and ensuring that when na
The profile update logic fails to keep display_name synchronized with name when the name field changes.
The bug is in update_profile: when first_name changes, full_name is not recomputed, leaving the derived field stale and violating the invariant full_name == first_name + ' ' + last_name. I fixed this
The profile update logic only recomputes full_name when last_name changes, so changing first_name leaves full_name stale and out of sync.

The bug is in update_profile: when the email changes, the code updates the email field but does not enforce the stated invariant that email changes must set verified to False and clear cached_greeting
Updating the email does not reset verification state or clear the cached greeting, so stale profile state persists after an email change.

The bug is a stale cache issue: update_product mutates the database but leaves any cached copy unchanged, so subsequent get_product calls can return outdated data. The fix is to invalidate the cache e
The cache is never invalidated when a product is updated, so get_product() can return stale cached data instead of the latest database state.

The catalog cache becomes stale because update_product() mutates the in-memory database but leaves any previously cached product entry untouched. As a result, get_product() can return the old cached c
The catalog updates the database but does not invalidate the cached product entry, so subsequent reads can return stale cached prices.

The stale data bug occurs because update_product() invalidates only the shared cache while api.get_product() checks the local cache first. If a product was previously cached locally, updates in the DB
Stale data is served because product updates invalidate only the shared cache, while the local cache can still return the old value first.

The bug is that _default_host is computed once at import time from _settings, so later calls to configure() or reset_settings() do not affect get_host(). To make the module reflect the current setting
The host value is cached at import time in `_default_host`, so later `configure()` or `reset_settings()` updates to `_settings['host']` are not reflected by `get_host()`.

The bug is that client.py eagerly caches the config at import time in _client_config. Because reset_config() only clears the config module's internal cache, the client keeps referencing the stale dict
The client caches the config at import time, so resetting or updating config later does not update the timeout used by the client.

The bug is that client.py caches the config object at import time in _client_cfg. When reset_config() clears config.py's singleton, subsequent calls from handler.py still use the stale imported dict b
The client captures a config dictionary at import time, so after reset_config() creates a fresh config object the client and handler continue reading the stale snapshot instead of the current config.

The queue module had a mutable default argument in enqueue(), causing the same list instance to be reused across calls and allowing tasks to accumulate unexpectedly. I refactored it to use None as the
The default queue parameter is a shared mutable list, causing tasks to persist across independent calls to enqueue().

The bug is caused by using a mutable default argument for seen in process_batch. That set persists across calls, so tasks processed in earlier batches are treated as duplicates in later batches and sk
The worker function uses a mutable default set for `seen`, causing task IDs to persist across separate calls and incorrectly skip valid tasks in later batches.

The bug is in scheduler.py: the decorator uses a module-level list as the default value for history, so every decorated function shares the same list. To fix this, each call to with_history should cre
The decorator uses a module-level mutable default list, causing all decorated scheduler functions to share the same history state.

The bug is that process_batch accumulates all item updates first and then records only one snapshot at the end, which violates the intended per-item snapshot behavior. I refactored the loop so each it
The batch processor updates the counter for all items first and records only one snapshot at the end, so it fails to capture a per-item snapshot after each increment.

The bug is in process_batch: the counter is incremented for every item, but emit_event is called only once after the loop, so only the last item produces an event. This makes the number of emitted eve
The batch processor emits only one event after the loop instead of one event per processed item, so event counts do not match the number of items processed.

`process_batch` records only one audit entry after the loop, so the audit log has fewer entries than the number of items processed.
The bug is that transform() only assigns _last_result when data is truthy, so empty/falsy inputs return the previous cached list instead of a fresh empty list. To make the behavior clear and correct,
The function returns a stale global list when the input is empty/falsy instead of always constructing and returning a fresh result list.

The loader leaves the previous status/data intact when given an empty or None source, so the pipeline reports stale results instead of an empty-call outcome.

The crash/incorrect behavior comes from pipeline.find_best using stale state when no record matches the threshold. If no valid record exceeds the threshold, the code falls into the else branch and ass
The pipeline returns stale data because `find_best` reuses `_last_best` when no current record matches, instead of resetting the result to `None`.
The bug is in retry_send: it keeps looping even after a successful send, and the existing condition is inverted for the intended behavior. Since send(msg) always succeeds in this codebase, retry_send
The retry loop never stops after a successful send because it checks only for failure and continues looping, causing duplicate sends despite success.

The duplicate messages are caused by sender.send_with_retry continuing its retry loop even after a successful send. Since send(msg) already appends the message to the store and notifies once, any extr
The retry loop keeps calling send() after a successful attempt because it never exits on success, causing duplicate stores and notifications.

The pipeline retries the sender without stopping after the first successful attempt, causing the same message to be sent and stored multiple times.

The order flow fails to release reserved inventory when wallet charging raises an error, leaving stock permanently reserved after a failed payment.

The bug is in order_service.place_order(): it reserves inventory before payment, but on payment failure it re-raises without releasing the reservation. This leaves inventory stuck in the reserved stat
Payment failure re-raises an exception without releasing the previously reserved inventory, leaving stock stuck in the reserved state.

Payment failures in place_order re-raise without rolling back the prior inventory reservation or removing the audit entry, leaving partial state behind.

The pipeline computes raw statistics from the normalized data instead of the original input, causing the reported raw metrics to be incorrect.

parsed.reasoning: The access-control mapping was missing the moderator role, which caused it to default to an empty permission set through the fallback in get_permissions(). I refactored the module for clarity by makin
The access control is incomplete because the role-to-permission mapping omits the "moderator" role, causing it to fall back to an empty permission set.

The bug is in the role dispatch table: the guest role exists in roles.py, but auth.py never maps "guest" to guest_access(). As a result, guest users fall back to _default_access() and receive no permi
Guest access is defined but never added to the role dispatch table, so guest users incorrectly fall back to the default no-access handler.

The bug is in auth.py: middleware already allows and marks service_account as an elevated, valid role, but authorize() does not include a service_account branch, so it falls through to the default den
Service accounts are authenticated by middleware but denied in authorization because auth.py lacks a service_account branch and defaults unknown roles to no access.

The limiter condition was incorrect: it used count > limit, which allows one extra request when count equals limit. Since the count represents the number of requests already made, the request should b
The limiter uses the wrong comparison in is_rate_limited, so it blocks only when count is greater than limit instead of when the limit has been reached.

The policy bug is in is_allowed(): it currently returns rate_ok or quota_ok, which permits a request when either the per-minute rate check or the daily quota check passes. That is too permissive and c
The policy uses logical OR instead of AND, so a request is allowed when either the rate check or quota check passes instead of requiring both.

The bug is that zero-amount payments return immediately before a ledger entry is created, violating the requirement that all payments must be recorded for audit compliance. I refactored the function t
The early return for zero-amount payments bypasses ledger recording, violating the requirement that every payment be audited.
The mismatch happens because process_payment returns early for duplicate txn_id values before calling ledger.record(). That means repeated calls are counted in payment logic but not in the ledger, so
Duplicate payment calls return early without recording to the ledger, so ledger entry count falls behind total process_payment invocations.

The bug is in payment.charge(): when a transaction is found in the cache, the function returns immediately before writing an audit entry. This causes cached charge calls to be missing from the audit l
The cached charge path returns before calling log_charge(), so repeated charges are missing audit entries even though they are correctly not re-recorded in the ledger.

The bug comes from maintaining two parallel lists for labels and values while insertion only updates _labels at a specific position and always appends to _values. This causes misalignment between labe
The report becomes misaligned because inserting a label at a specific position does not insert the corresponding value at the same index, so the parallel arrays drift out of sync.
The bug is in Report.delete_column: it deletes the header but leaves each row unchanged, so headers and row values become misaligned after a column removal. The fix is to delete the same column index
Deleting a column only updates the headers and leaves each row unchanged, causing header/row length mismatch and misaligned rendered data.

The formatting breaks because insert_column updates headers and rows but leaves column_widths unchanged, so the table components become out of sync. This causes validation to fail and the formatter to
The report breaks after inserting a column because insert_column updates headers and rows but leaves column_widths at the old length, causing formatter alignment to use mismatched metadata.

The bug is that callers may provide camelCase flag names while the underlying dictionary uses snake_case keys, and the current implementation silently falls back to False on missing keys. I refactored
Feature flag lookups fail because callers use camelCase names while the stored keys are snake_case, so direct dictionary access with a default silently returns False for valid flags.

The analytics flag was always resolving to the default because is_analytics_enabled() used the wrong dot-path: 'features.analytics.enabled' instead of the actual config path 'feature.analytics.enabled
The analytics flag lookup uses the wrong top-level key path, so traversal misses the enabled value and falls back to False.

The bug is in the environment variable name mapping for the dark_mode flag: it points to FEATURE_DARKMODE instead of FEATURE_DARK_MODE, so the env override is never found. I also tightened the flag lo
The feature flag lookup uses an incorrect environment variable name for dark_mode and only returns True for env values, so the env override is effectively ignored when the intended key is present.
